selector.exact: but creators that absorb. Being has been confused with waking-being

annotations.anchor: It is not products that 'influence,' but creators that absorb.
annotations.note: The methodological heart of the chapter, and a direct attack on the way history is usually written. 'Influence,' 'continuity,' 'permanent effects' — the causal chains beloved of the nineteenth century — are, for Spengler, illusions. Cultures do not transmit forms to one another like billiard balls; a young Culture reaches into the dead form-stock of an older one and takes only the few things its own soul could already have invented. The creator absorbs; the product does not act. This is what makes each Culture a sealed monad and licenses treating them as separate organisms.

The sealed-monad thesis at its sharpest. Indian and Chinese Buddhists used the same words, rites, and symbols, yet were 'spiritually as far apart as ever' — 'two different souls, each going its own way.' Spengler insists that meaning never crosses the gulf between Cultures intact; the same scripture, the same Aristotle, the same Christ is silently re-made by each soul that receives it. Bear this in mind as a working assumption of his whole comparative method — and as one of its most disputable premises.

selector.exact: Every relation that was accepted was not only an exception, but also a misunderstanding

annotations.anchor: Every relation that was accepted was … a misunderstanding
annotations.note: A wonderfully counter-intuitive idea: borrowing is creative misreading. The Renaissance did not absorb antiquity but 'misunderstood' it into something Faustian; 'the inner force of a Being is never so clearly evidenced as it is in this art of deliberate misunderstanding.' For Spengler, the more reverently a Culture praises an alien thought, 'the more fundamentally in truth we have denatured it.' What looks like fidelity to the past is really the strength of a present will-to-form.

selector.exact: Greek, Arabian, and Gothic—who had not one concept or thought in common

annotations.anchor: the 'three Aristotles' — Greek, Arabian, and Gothic — who had not one concept or thought in common
annotations.note: Spengler's favourite example of the sealed monad. One author, Aristotle, became three incommensurable philosophies — the Greek Aristotle of the Polis, the Magian Aristotle of the Arabian commentators, and the Gothic Aristotle of the Schoolmen — 'who had not one concept or thought in common.' Likewise 'the' Christian religion, he argues, was a different thing for Magian and for Faustian man. The continuity is in the name only; the meaning is re-created each time.

selector.exact: passed through three whole courses of evolution in three Cultures, with completely different meanings in each

annotations.anchor: Roman law … passed through three whole courses of evolution in three Cultures
annotations.note: Spengler turns to law to clinch the argument, because law looks like the most universal and transmissible thing of all. His claim: Roman law only appears to have travelled unchanged for two millennia; in fact it lived three separate lives — Classical, Magian (the Eastern Roman / Arabian jurists), and Faustian — meaning something different in each. The next notes trace the deepest of these contrasts, between the Classical and the Western legal soul.

selector.exact: Classical law was a law of bodies, while ours is a law of functions.

annotations.anchor: Classical law was a law of bodies, while ours is a law of functions.
annotations.note: The morphology of law in one sentence, and a perfect echo of Spengler's prime-symbols. Classical (Apollinian) law is a 'juristic statics' of bodies present-at-hand: the 'persona' is a body in the Polis, the slave a 'thing,' law made case-by-case for the moment. Western (Faustian) law is a 'juristic dynamics' — 'persons are not bodies, but units of force and will' — striving toward a complete code that decides 'every conceivable future problem.' Classical law bears 'the stamp of the moment,' Western law 'the stamp of the future.' Statics vs. dynamics, body vs. force: the same opposition that divides the Doric temple from the Gothic cathedral.

annotations.anchor: the theft of electric power … had to be dealt with under an ad hoc statute
annotations.note: A concrete, almost prophetic illustration. Around 1900 Western law could not comfortably handle the theft of electric power, patents, copyright, or 'intellectual property' — because these are not 'things' (bodies) but forces, functions, and potentialities. Spengler reads this strain as proof that the Faustian soul has outgrown the Roman 'law of things' and demands a law of forces. It is a striking moment where his morphology touches problems we still litigate.

selector.exact: The Romans created a juristic statics; our task is juristic dynamics.

annotations.anchor: The Romans created a juristic statics; our task is juristic dynamics.
annotations.note: It is demonstrations like this one — showing that even law, seemingly the most universal of institutions, bears the physiognomic stamp of its Culture — that scholars have found the durable part of Spengler. According to H. Stuart Hughes, Spengler's lasting value lies not in his predictions or his system — whose method Hughes criticized at length — but exactly in this comparative morphology of cultural style, the trained eye for the inner form shared by a Culture's art, mathematics, law, and politics. Read the chapter for that eye, whatever you make of the metaphysics around it.
